greek_head: Ὁ νόμος ἡμῖν τί ἐστιν Ὁποῖον καὶ ἐρωτᾷς τῶν νόμων
greek_tokens: o nomos egw tis eimi opoios kai erwtaw o nomos tis de eimi ostis diaferw nomos nomos kata autos outos kata o nomos eimi skopaw gar dh o tugxanw erwtaw su erwtaw gar wsper ei anairw tis eimi xrusos ei egw wsautws anairw opoios kai legos xrusos oiomai su ou an orqos eromai oudeis gar pou diaferw oute xrusos
english_head: Tell me, what is law? To what kind of law does your question refer? What! Is there any difference between law and law, in this particular point of being law? For just consider what is the actual question I am putting to you. It is as though I had asked, what is gold: if you had asked me in the same manner, to what kind of gold I refer, I think your question would have been incorrect. For I presume there is no difference between gold and gold,

greek_head: χρυσοῦ οὔτε λίθος λίθου κατά γε τὸ λίθος εἶναι καὶ κατὰ τὸ χρυσός οὕτω δὲ οὐδὲ νόμος που νόμου οὐδὲν διαφέρει
greek_tokens: xruseos oute liqos liqos kata ge o liqos eimi kai kata o xrusos outws de oude nomos pou nomos oudeis diaferw alla pas eimi tautos nomos gar ekastos autos eimi omoios ou o men mallon o de hsswn outos dh autos erwtaw o pas tis eimi nomos ei oun su proxeiron eipon tis oun allos nomos eaw an eimi swkraths alla h o nomizw eimi kai logos su dokew eimi o legw h oyis o oraw h akoh o akouw h allos men logos allos
english_head: or between stone and stone, in point of being gold or stone; and so neither does law differ at all from law, I suppose, but they are all the same thing. For each of them is law alike, not one more so, and another less. That is the particular point of my question—what is law as a whole? So if you are ready, tell me. Well, what else should law be, Socrates, but things loyally accepted? And so speech, you think, is the things that are spoken, or sight the things seen, or hearing the things heard? Or is speech

greek_head: δὲ τὰ λεγόμενα καὶ ἄλλο μὲν ὄψις ἄλλο δὲ τὰ ὁρώμενα καὶ ἄλλο μὲν ἀκοή ἄλλο δὲ τὰ ἀκουόμενα καὶ ἄλλο δὴ
greek_tokens: de o legw kai allos men oyis allos de o oraw kai allos men akoh allos de o akouw kai allos dh nomos allos de o nomizw outws h pws su dokew allos egw nun fainw ou ara nomos eimi o nomizw ou egw dokew tis deomai an eaw nomos episkeptomai autos wde ei tis egw o nundi legw anairw epei oraw fatos
english_head: something distinct from the things spoken, sight something distinct from the things seen, and hearing something distinct from the things heard; and so law is something distinct from things loyally accepted? Is this so, or what is your view? I find it now to be something distinct. Then law is not things loyally accepted. I think not. Now what can law be? Let us consider it in this way. Suppose someone had asked us about what was stated just now:

greek_head: τὰ ὁρώμενα ὁρᾶσθαι τίνι ὄντι τῇ ὄψει ὁρᾶται ἀπεκρινάμεθ' ἂν αὐτῷ ὅτι αἰσθήσει ταύτῃ τῇ διὰ τῶν ὀφθαλμῶν δηλούσῃ
greek_tokens: o oraw oraw tis eimi o oraw oraw apokrinw an autos ostis aisqhsis outos o dia o ofqalmos dhlow o pragma ei de au airw egw tis de epei akoh o akouw akouw tis eimi o akoh apokrinw an autos ostis aisqhsis outos o dia o aatos dhlow egw o fwnh outws toinun kai ei aneromai egw epei nomos o nomizw nomizw tis eimi o nomos nomizw
english_head: Since you say it is by sight that things seen are seen, what is this sight whereby they are seen? Our answer to him would have been: That sensation which shows objects by means of the eyes. And if he had asked us again: Well then, since it is by hearing that things heard are heard, what is hearing? Our answer to him would have been: That sensation which shows us sounds by means of the ears. In the same way then, suppose he should also ask us: Since it is by law that loyally accepted things are so accepted, what is this law whereby they are so accepted?

greek_head: πότερον αἰσθήσει τινὶ ἢ δηλώσει ὥσπερ τὰ μανθανόμενα μανθάνεται δηλούσῃ τῇ ἐπιστήμῃ ἢ εὑρέσει τινί ὥσπερ τὰ
greek_tokens: poteros aisqhsis tis h dhlwsis wsper o manqanw manqanw dhlow o episthmh h euresis tis wsper o euriskw euriskw oios o men ugieinos kai noswdhs iatrikos o de e qeos dianoeomai os fhmi e mantis mantikos o gar pou texnh egw euresis eimi o pragma eimi gar panu ge tis oun an outos upolambanw malista o nomos eimi o dogma outos kai yhfisma egw dokew tis gar an allos tis fhmi nomos eimi wste kinduneuw o
english_head: Is it some sensation or showing, as when things learnt are learnt by knowledge showing them, or some discovery, as when things discovered are discovered—for instance, the causes of health and sickness by medicine, or the designs of the gods, as the prophets say, by prophecy; for art is surely our discovery of things, is it not? Certainly. Then what thing especially of this sort shall we surmise law to be? Our resolutions and decrees, I imagine: for how else can one describe law?

greek_head: σὺ ἐρωτᾷς τὸ ὅλον τοῦτο νόμος δόγμα πόλεως εἶναι Δόξαν ὡς ἔοικε λέγεις πολιτικὴν τὸν νόμον
greek_tokens: su erwtaw o oloc outos nomos dogma polis eimi doca ws eoika legw politikos o nomos egw kai isos kalos legw taxa de wde ameinwn eimi legw tis sofos egw oukoun e sofos eimi sofia sofos nai tis de e dikaios dikaiosunh dikaios panu ge oukoun kai e nomimos nomos nomimos nai e de
english_head: So that apparently the whole thing, law, as you put it in your question, is a city’s resolution. State opinion, it seems, is what you call law. I do. And perhaps you are right: but I fancy we shall get a better knowledge in this way. You call some men wise? I do. And the wise are wise by wisdom? Yes. And again, the just are just by justice? Certainly. And so the law-abiding are law-abiding by law?

greek_head: ἄνομοι ἀνομίᾳ ἄνομοι Ναί Οἱ δὲ νόμιμοι δίκαιοι Ναί Οἱ δὲ ἄνομοι ἄδικοι Ἄδικοι
greek_tokens: anomos anomia anomos nai e de nomimos dikaios nai e de anomos adikos adikos oukoun kalos o dikaiosunh te kai o nomos outws aisxros de o adikia te kai o anomia nai kai o men swzw o polis kai allos pas o de apollumi kai anatrepw nai ws peri kalew ara tis eimi deomai o nomos dianoeomai kai ws agaqos autos zhtew pws de ou oukoun dogma fhmi eimi polis o nomos
english_head: Yes. And the lawless are lawless by lawlessness? Yes. And the law-abiding are just? Yes. And the lawless are unjust? Unjust. And justice and law are most noble? That is so. And injustice and lawlessness most base? Yes. And the former preserve cities and everything else, while the latter destroy and overturn them? Yes. Hence we must regard law as something noble, and seek after it as a good. Undeniably. And we said that law is a city’s resolution?

greek_head: Ἔφαμεν γάρ Τί οὖν οὐκ ἔστιν τὰ μὲν χρηστὰ δόγματα τὰ δὲ πονηρά Ἔστιν μὲν οὖν Καὶ
greek_tokens: fhmi gar tis oun ou eimi o men xrhstos dogma o de ponhros eimi men oun kai meis nomos ge ou eimi ponhros ou gar ou ara orqos exis apokrinw outws aplos ostis nomos eimi dogma polis ou egw dokew ou ara armozw an o ponhros dogma nomos eimi ou dhta alla meis doca ge tis kai autos egw katafainw o nomos eimi epei de ou o ponhros doca ara ou hdh outos katadhlos ws o xrhstos eiper doca nomos eimi nai doca de xrhstos tis eimi ou o alhqhs
english_head: So we did. Well now, are not some resolutions good, and others evil? Yes, to be sure. And, you know, law was not evil. No, indeed. So it is not right to reply, in that simple fashion, that law is a city’s resolution. I agree that it is not. An evil resolution, you see, cannot properly be a law. No, to be sure. But still, I am quite clear myself that law is some sort of opinion; and since it is not evil opinion, is it not manifest by this time that it is good opinion, granting that law is opinion? Yes. But what is good opinion? Is it not true opinion? Yes.

greek_head: Ναί Οὐκοῦν ἡ ἀληθὴς δόξα τοῦ ὄντος ἐστὶν ἐξεύρεσις Ἔστιν γάρ Ὁ νόμος ἄρα βούλεται
greek_tokens: nai oukoun o alhqhs doca o eimi eimi eceuresis eimi gar o nomos ara boulomai o eimi eimi eceuresis pws oun eimi swkraths ei o nomos eimi o eimi eceuresis ou aei o autos nomos xraomai peri o autos ei o eimi ge egw eceuriskw boulomai men oudeis hsswn o nomos eimi o eimi eceuresis e de ara mh o autos aei nomos xraomai
english_head: And true opinion is discovery of reality? Yes, it is. So law tends to be discovery of reality. Then how is it, Socrates, if law is discovery of reality, that we do not use always the same laws on the same matters, if we have thus got realities discovered? Law tends none the less to be discovery of reality: but men, who do not use

greek_head: ἄνθρωποι ὡς δοκοῦμεν οὐκ ἀεὶ δύνανται ἐξευρίσκειν ὃ βούλεται ὁ νόμος τὸ ὄν ἐπεὶ φέρε ἴδωμεν ἐὰν ἄρα ἡμῖν
greek_tokens: anqrwpos ws dokew ou aei dunamai eceuriskw o boulomai o nomos o eimi epei ferw eidon ean ara egw enqende katadhlos gignomai eite o autos aei nomos xraomai h allote allos kai ei apas o autos h allos allos alla outos ge eimi swkraths ou xalepos gignwskw ostis oute e autos aei o autos nomos xraomai allos te allos epei autika egw men ou nomos eimi anqrwpos quw alla anosios karxhdonios de quw ws osios eimi
english_head: always the same laws, as we observe, are not always able to discover what the law is intent on—reality. For come now, let us see if from this point onward we can get it clear whether we use always the same laws or different ones at different times, and whether we all use the same, or some of us use some, and others others. Why, that, Socrates, is no difficult matter to determine—that the same men do not use always the same laws, and also that different men use different ones. With us, for instance, human sacrifice is not legal, but unholy,

greek_head: καὶ νόμιμον αὐτοῖς καὶ ταῦτα ἔνιοι αὐτῶν καὶ τοὺς αὑτῶν ὑεῖς τῷ Κρόνῳ ὡς ἴσως καὶ σὺ ἀκήκοας καὶ μὴ ὅτι βάρβαροι
greek_tokens: kai nomimos autos kai outos enioi autos kai o eautou uios o kronos ws isos kai su akouw kai mh ostis barbaros anqrwpos egw allos nomos xraomai alla kai e en o lukaios outos kai e o aqamas ekgonos oios qusia quw ellhn eimi wsper kai egw autos oida pou kai autos akouw oios nomos xraomai pro o peri o apoqnhskw iereion te prosfazw pro o ekfora o nekros kai egxutistria metapempw e
english_head: whereas the Carthaginians perform it as a thing they account holy and legal, and that too when some of them sacrifice even their own sons to Cronos, as I daresay you yourself have heard. And not merely is it foreign peoples who use different laws from ours, but our neighbors in Lycaea and the descendants of Athamas —you know their sacrifices, Greeks though they be. And as to ourselves too, you know, of course, from what you have heard yourself, the kind of laws we formerly used in regard to our dead, when we slaughtered sacred victims before

greek_head: δ' αὖ ἐκείνων ἔτι πρότεροι αὐτοῦ καὶ ἔθαπτον ἐν τῇ οἰκίᾳ τοὺς ἀποθανόντας ἡμεῖς δὲ τούτων οὐδὲν ποιοῦμεν μυρία
greek_tokens: de au ekeinos eti proteros autos kai qaptw en o oikia o apoqnhskw egw de outos oudeis poiew murios de an tis exw toioutos eipon polus gar euruxwria o apodeicis ws oute egw egw autos aei kata tautos nomizw oute allhlwn e anqrwpos oudeis su qaumastos eimi eimi beltistos ei su men orqos legw egw de outos lanqanw alla ews an su te kata sautou legos o su dokew makros logos kai palin egw
english_head: the funeral procession, and engaged urn-women to collect the bones from the ashes. Then again, a yet earlier generation used to bury the dead where they were, in the house: but we do none of these things. One might give thousands of other instances; for there is ample means of proving that neither we copy ourselves nor mankind each other always in laws and customs. And it is no wonder, my excellent friend, if what you say is correct, and I have overlooked it. But if you continue to express your views after your own fashion in lengthy speeches,

greek_head: οὐδὲν μή ποτε συμβῶμεν ὡς ἐγὼ οἶμαι ἐὰν δὲ κοινὸν τεθῇ τὸ σκέμμα τάχ' ἂν ὁμολογήσαιμεν εἰ μὲν οὖν βούλει
greek_tokens: oudeis mh pote sumbainw ws egw oimh ean de koinos tiqhmi o skemma taxa an omologew ei men oun boulomai punqanomai tis para egw koinh meta egw skopaw ei de au boulomai apokrinw alla eqelw eimi swkraths apokrinw ostis an boulomai ferw dh su poteros nomizw o dikaios adikos eimi kai o adikos dikaios h o men dikaios dikaios o de adikos adikos egw men o te dikaios dikaios kai o adikos
english_head: and I speak likewise, we shall never come to any agreement, in my opinion: but if we study the matter jointly, we may perhaps concur. Well now, if you like, hold a joint inquiry with me by asking me questions; or if you prefer, by answering them. Why, I am willing, Socrates, to answer anything you like. Come then, do you consider just things to be unjust and unjust things just, or just things to be just and unjust things unjust? I consider just things to be just, and unjust things unjust.

greek_head: ἄδικα Οὐκοῦν καὶ παρὰ πᾶσιν οὕτως ὡς ἐνθάδε νομίζεται Ναί Οὐκοῦν καὶ ἐν Πέρσαις
greek_tokens: adikos oukoun kai para pas outws ws enqade nomizw nai oukoun kai en perqw kai en perqw alla aei dhpou aei poteros de o plew elkw barus nomizw enqade o de elasswn koufos h enantion ou alla o plew elkw barus o de elasswn koufos oukoun kai en karxhdwn kai en lukaios nai o men kalos ws eoika pantaxou nomizw
english_head: And are they so considered among all men elsewhere as they are here? Yes. And among the Persians also? Among the Persians also. Always, I presume? Always. Are things that weigh more considered heavier here, and things that weigh less lighter, or the contrary? No, those that weigh more are considered heavier, and those that weigh less lighter. And is it so in Carthage also, and in Lycaea? Yes. Noble things, it would seem, are everywhere considered noble,

greek_head: καλὰ καὶ τὰ αἰσχρὰ αἰσχρά ἀλλ' οὐ τὰ αἰσχρὰ καλὰ οὐδὲ τὰ καλὰ αἰσχρά Οὕτως Οὐκοῦν ὡς κατὰ πάντων
greek_tokens: kalos kai o aisxros aisxros alla ou o aisxros kalos oude o kalos aisxros outws oukoun ws kata pas eipon o eimi nomizw eimi ou o mh eimi kai para egw kai para o allos apas egw dokew os an ara o eimi amartanw o nomimos amartanw outws men eimi swkraths ws su legw kai fainw outos nomimos kai egw aei kai o allos epeidan de ennoew
english_head: and base things base; not base things noble or noble things base. That is so. And thus, as a universal rule, realities, and not unrealities, are accepted as real, both among us and among all other men. I agree. Then whoever fails to attain reality, fails to attain accepted law. In your present way of putting it, Socrates, the same things appear to be accepted as lawful both by us and by the rest of the world, always:

greek_head: ὅτι οὐδὲν παυόμεθα ἄνω κάτω μετατιθέμενοι τοὺς νόμους οὐ δύναμαι πεισθῆναι
greek_tokens: ostis oudeis pauw anoos katos metatiqhmi o nomos ou dunamai peiqw isos gar ou ennoew outos metapesseuw ostis tautos eimi alla wde meta egw autos aqrew hdh pote entugxanw suggramma peri ugieia o kamnw egw oida oun tis texnh outos eimi o suggramma oida ostis iatrikos oukoun iatros kalew o episthmwn peri outos fhmi
english_head: but when I reflect that we are continually changing our laws in all sorts of ways, I cannot bring myself to assent. Perhaps it is because you do not reflect that when we change our pieces at draughts they are the same pieces. But look at it, as I do, in this way. Have you in your time come across a treatise on healing the sick? I have. Then do you know to what art such a treatise belongs? I do: medicine. And you give the name of doctors to those who have knowledge of these matters? Yes.

greek_head: Πότερον οὖν οἱ ἐπιστήμονες ταὐτὰ περὶ τῶν αὐτῶν νομίζουσιν ἢ ἄλλοι ἄλλα Ταὐτὰ ἔμοιγε δοκοῦσι
greek_tokens: poteros oun e episthmwn tautos peri o autos nomizw h allos allos tautos egw dokew poteros e ellhn monos o ellhn h kai e barbaros eautou te kai o ellhn peri os an oida tautos nomizw tautos dhpou polus anagkh eimi o oida autos eautou sunnomizw kai ellhn kai barbaros kalos ge apokrinw oukoun kai aei nai kai aei oukoun kai e iatros suggrafw peri ugieia o
english_head: Then do those who have knowledge accept the same views on the same things, or do they accept different views? The same, in my opinion. Do Greeks only accept the same views as Greeks on what they know, or do foreigners also agree on these matters, both among themselves and with Greeks? It is quite inevitable, I should say, that those who know should agree in accepting the same views, whether Greeks or foreigners. Well answered. And do they so always? Yes, it is so always. And do doctors on their part, in their treatises on health,

greek_head: καὶ νομίζουσιν εἶναι Ναί Ἰατρικὰ ἄρα καὶ ἰατρικοὶ νόμοι ταῦτα τὰ συγγράμματα ἐστὶν τὰ τῶν ἰατρῶν
greek_tokens: kai nomizw eimi nai iatrikos ara kai iatrikos nomos outos o suggramma eimi o o iatros iatrikos mentoi ara oun kai o gewrgikos suggramma gewrgikos nomos eimi nai tinw oun eimi o peri khpos ergasia suggramma kai nomimos khpourew khpourikos ara nomos egw eimi outos nai o efisthmi khpos arxw pws de ou efisthmi de e khpouros nai tinw de o peri oyon skeuasia suggramma te kai nomimos mageiros mageirikos ara outos nomos eimi mageirikos o efisthmi ws
english_head: write what they accept as real? Yes. Then these treatises of the doctors are medical, and medical laws. Medical, to be sure. And are agricultural treatises likewise agricultural laws? Yes. And whose are the treatises and accepted rules about garden-work? Gardeners’. So these are our gardening laws. Yes. Of people who know how to control gardens? Certainly. And it is the gardeners who know. Yes. And whose are the treatises and accepted rules about the confection of tasty dishes? Cooks’. Then there are laws of cookery? Of cookery. Of people who know, it would seem, how to control the confection of tasty dishes?

greek_head: ἔοικεν ὄψου σκευασίας ἄρχειν Ναί Ἐπίστανται δέ ὥς φασιν οἱ μάγειροι Ἐπίστανται γάρ
greek_tokens: eoika oyon skeuasia arxw nai efisthmi de os fhmi e mageiros efisthmi gar eien tinw de dh o peri polis dioikhsis suggramma te kai nomimos eimi ara ou o efisthmi polis arxw egw dokew efisthmi de allos tis h e politikos te kai e basilikos outos men oun politikos ara outos suggramma eimi os e anqrwpos nomos kalew basilh te kai androw
english_head: Yes. And it is the cooks, they say, who know? Yes, it is they who know. Very well; and now, whose are the treatises and accepted rules about the government of a state? Of the people who know how to control states, are they not? I agree. And is it anyone else than statesmen and royal persons who know? It is they, to be sure. Then what people call laws are treatises of state,—

greek_head: ἀγαθῶν συγγράμματα Ἀληθῆ λέγεις Ἄλλο τι οὖν οἵ γε ἐπιστάμενοι οὐκ ἄλλοτε ἄλλα
greek_tokens: agaqos suggramma alhqhs legw allos tis oun e ge efisthmi ou allote allos suggrafw peri o autos ou oude metaqew pote peri o autos eteros kai eteros nomimos ou dhta ean oun oraw tis opou outos poiew poteros fhmi episthmwn eimi h anepisthmwn o outos poiew anepisthmwn oukoun kai o men an orqos eimi nomimos autos fhmi ekastos eimi h o iatrikos h o mageirikos h o khpourikos
english_head: writings of kings and good men. That is true. And must it not be that those who know will not write differently at different times on the same matters? They will not. Nor will they ever change one set of accepted rules for another in respect of the same matters. No, indeed. So if we see some persons anywhere doing this, shall we say that those who do so have knowledge, or have none? That they have no knowledge. And again, whatever is right, we shall say is lawful for each person, whether in medicine or in cookery or in gardening? Yes.

greek_head: Ναί Ὃ δ' ἂν μὴ ὀρθὸν ᾖ οὐκέτι φήσομεν τοῦτο νόμιμον εἶναι Οὐκέτι Ἄνομον ἄρα γίγνεται
greek_tokens: nai o de an mh orqos eimi ouketi fhmi outos nomimos eimi ouketi anomos ara gignomai anagkh oukoun kai en o suggramma o peri o dikaios kai adikos kai oloc peri polis diakosmhsis te kai peri o ws xrh polis dioikew o men orqos nomos eimi basilikos o de mh orqos ou o dokew nomos eimi o mh oida eimi gar anomos nai
english_head: And whatever is not right we shall decline to call lawful? We shall decline. Then it becomes unlawful. It must. And again, in writings about what is just and unjust, and generally about the government of a state and the proper way of governing it, that which is right is the king’s law, but not so that which is not right, though it seems to be law to those who do not know; for it is unlawful. Yes.

greek_head: Ὀρθῶς ἄρα ὡμολογήσαμεν νόμον εἶναι τοῦ ὄντος εὕρεσιν Φαίνεται
greek_tokens: orqos ara omologew nomos eimi o eimi euresis fainw eti de kai ode en autos diaqew tis episthmos dianemw epi gaia o sperma gewrgos outos de o acios sperma ekastos gaia dianemw nai o gewrgos ara nomeus agaqos outos kai e outos nomos kai dianomh epi outos orqos eimi nai tis de krouma epi o melh agaqos nomeus kai o acios nemw kai e tis nomos orqos eimi e o
english_head: Then we rightly admitted that law is discovery of reality. So it appears. Now let us observe this further point about it. Who has knowledge of distributing seed over land? A farmer. And does he distribute the suitable seed to each sort of land? Yes. Then the farmer is a good apportioner of it, and his laws and distributions are right in this matter? Yes. And who is a good apportioner of notes struck for a tune, skilled in distributing suitable notes, and who is it whose laws are right here?

greek_head: αὐλητοῦ καὶ τοῦ κιθαριστοῦ Ὁ νομικώτατος ἄρα ἐν τούτοις οὗτος αὐλητικώτατος Ναί Τίς δὲ τὴν
greek_tokens: aulhths kai o kiqarisths o nomikos ara en outos outos aulhtikos nai tis de o trofh epi o o anqrwpos swma dianemw aristos ou os o acios nai o outos ara dianomh kai e nomos beltistos kai ostis peri outos nomikos kai nomeus aristos panu ge tis outos
english_head: The flute-player and the harp-player. Then he who is the best lawyer in these matters is the best flute-player. Yes. And who is most skilled in distributing food to human bodies? Is it not he who assigns suitable food? Yes. Then his distributions and laws are best, and whoever is the best lawyer in this matter is also the best apportioner. Certainly. Who is he? A trainer.

greek_head: Παιδοτρίβης Οὗτος τὴν ἀνθρωπείαν ἀγέλην τοῦ σώματος νέμειν κράτιστος Ναί Τίς δὲ τὴν τῶν
greek_tokens: paidotribhs outos o anqrwpeios agelh o swma nemw kratistos nai tis de o o probaton agelh kratistos nemw tis onoma autos poimhn e o poimhn ara nomos aristos o probaton nai e de o boukolos o bous nai e de o tis nomos aristos o yuxh o anqrwpos ou e o basileus fhmi fhmi dh
english_head: He is the best man to pasture the human herd of the body? Yes. And who is the best man to pasture a flock of sheep? What is his name? A shepherd. Then the shepherd’s laws are best for sheep. Yes. And the herdsman’s for oxen. Yes. And whose laws are best for the souls of men? The king’s, are they not? Say if you agree. I do.

greek_head: Καλῶς τοίνυν λέγεις ἔχοις ἂν οὖν εἰπεῖν τίς τῶν παλαιῶν ἀγαθὸς γέγονεν ἐν τοῖς αὐλητικοῖς νόμοις νομοθέτης
greek_tokens: kalos toinun legw exw an oun eipon tis o palaios agaqos gignomai en o aulhtikos nomos nomoqeths isos ou ennoew alla egw boulomai su upomimnhskw panu men oun ara oun o marsuhs legw kai o paidikon autos olumpos o fruc alhqhs legw outos dh kai o aulhma qeios eimi kai monos kinew kai ekfainw o o qea en xreia eimi kai eti kai
english_head: Then you are quite right. Now can you tell me who, in former times, has proved himself a good lawgiver in regard to the laws of flute-playing? Perhaps you cannot think of him: would you like me to remind you? Do by all means. Then is it Marsyas, by tradition, and his beloved Olympus, the Phrygian? That is true. And their flute-tunes also are most divine, and alone stir and make manifest those who are in need of the gods; and to this day they only remain, as being divine.

greek_head: νῦν μόνα λοιπά ὡς θεῖα ὄντα Ἔστι ταῦτα Τίς δὲ λέγεται τῶν παλαιῶν βασιλέων ἀγαθὸς νομοθέτης γεγονέναι
greek_tokens: nun monos loipas ws qeios eimi eimi outos tis de legw o palaios basilh agaqos nomoqeths gignomai e eti kai nun o nomimos menos ws qeios eimi ou ennoew ou oida tinw palaios nomos xraomai o ellhn ara lakedaimonios legw kai lukourgos o nomoqeths alla outos ge oudepw isos eths triakosioi h oligos outos plew alla outos o nomimos o beltistos pinw
english_head: That is so. And who by tradition has shown himself a good lawgiver among the ancient kings, so that to this day his ordinances remain, as being divine? I cannot think. Do you not know which of the Greeks use the most ancient laws? Do you mean the Spartans, and Lycurgus the lawgiver? Why, that is a matter, I daresay, of less than three hundred years ago, or but a little more. But whence is it that

greek_head: ἥκει οἶσθα Φασί γε ἐκ Κρήτης
greek_tokens: hkw oida fhmi ge ek krhth oukoun outos palaios nomos xraomai o ellhn nai oida oun tinw outos agaqos basileus eimi minws kai radamanqus e zeus kai eurwph pais os ode eimi e nomos radamanqun ge fhmi eimi swkraths dikaios anhr o de minws agrios tis kai xalepos kai adikos attikos eimi beltistos legw muqos kai tragikos
english_head: the best of those ordinances come? Do you know? From Crete, so they say. Then the people there use the most ancient laws in Greece? Yes. Then do you know who were their good kings? Minos and Rhadamanthus, the sons of Zeus and Europa; those laws were theirs. Rhadamanthus, they do say, Socrates, was a just man; but Minos was a savage sort of person, harsh and unjust. Your tale, my excellent friend, is a fiction of Attic tragedy.

greek_head: Τί δέ οὐ ταῦτα λέγεται περὶ Μίνω Οὔκουν ὑπό γε Ὁμήρου καὶ Ἡσιόδου καίτοι γε
greek_tokens: tis de ou outos legw peri minws oukoun upo ge omhros kai hsiodos kaitoi ge piqanos eimi h sumpas e tragwdopoios os su akouw outos legw alla tis meis outos peri minws legw egw dh su eraomai ina mh kai su wsper e polus asebew ou gar esqw ostis outos asebhs eimi oude ostis xrh mallon eulabeomai plhn eis qeos kai logos kai ergon ecamartanw deuteros de eis o qeios anqrwpos alla panu polus xrh promhqeia poiew aei otan mellw
english_head: What! Is not this the tradition about Minos? Not in Homer and Hesiod; and yet they are more to be believed than all the tragedians together, from whom you heard your tale. Well, and what, pray, is their tale about Minos? I will tell you, in order that you may not share the impiety of the multitude: for there cannot conceivably be anything more impious or more to be guarded against than being mistaken in word and deed with regard to the gods, and after them, with regard to divine men; you must take very great precaution, whenever you are about to

greek_head: ἄνδρα ψέξειν ἢ ἐπαινέσεσθαι μὴ οὐκ ὀρθῶς εἴπῃς τούτου καὶ ἕνεκα χρὴ μανθάνειν διαγιγνώσκειν χρηστοὺς καὶ πονηροὺς
greek_tokens: anhr yegw h epainew mh ou orqos eipon outos kai eneka xrh manqanw diagignwskw xrhstos kai ponhros anhr nemesaw gar o qeos otan tis yegw o eautou omoios h epainew o eautou enantios exw eimi de outos o agaqos mh gar tis oiomai liqos men eimi ieros kai culon kai orneon kai ofis anqrwpos de mh alla pas outos ieros eimi anqrwpos o agaqos kai miaros o ponhros hdh oun kai peri minws ws autos omhros te kai hsiodos
english_head: blame or praise a man, so as not to speak incorrectly. For this reason you must learn to distinguish honest and dishonest men: for God feels resentment when one blames a man who is like himself, or praises a man who is the opposite; and the former is the good man. For you must not suppose that while stocks and stones and birds and snakes are sacred, men are not; nay, the good man is the most sacred of all these things, and the wicked man is the most defiled. So if I now proceed to relate how Minos is eulogized by Homer

greek_head: ἐγκωμιάζουσι τούτου ἕνεκα φράσω ἵνα μὴ ἄνθρωπος ὢν ἀνθρώπου εἰς ἥρω Διὸς ὑὸν λόγῳ ἐξαμαρτάνῃς Ὅμηρος
greek_tokens: egkwmiazw outos eneka frazw ina mh anqrwpos eimi anqrwpos eis hrws zeus uios logos ecamartanw omhros gar peri krhth legos ostis polus anqrwpos en autos eimi kai enenhkonta polis o de fhmi en knwssos megas polis enqa te minws ennewros basileuw zeus megas oarisths
english_head: and Hesiod, my purpose is to prevent you, a man sprung from a man, from making a mistake in regard to a hero who was the son of Zeus. For Homer, in telling of Crete that there were in it many men and ninety cities, says: And amongst them is the mighty city of Cnossos, where Minos was king, having colloquy with mighty Zeus in the ninth year. Hom. Od. 19.179

greek_head: ἔστιν οὖν τοῦτο Ὁμήρου ἐγκώμιον εἰς Μίνων διὰ βραχέων εἰρημένον οἷον οὐδ' εἰς ἕνα τῶν ἡρώων ἐποίησεν Ὅμηρος
greek_tokens: eimi oun outos omhros egkwmios eis minws dia braxea erw oios oude eis enos o hrws poiew omhros ostis men gar o zeus sofisths eimi kai o texnh outos pagkalos eimi pollaxou kai alloqi dhlow atar kai entauqa legw gar o minws suggignomai enatos etos o zeus en logos kai foiths paideuw ws upo sofisths eimi o zeus ostis oun outos o geras ou eimi ostis aponemw omhros o hrws upo zeus paideuw allos h minws
english_head: Now here in Homer we have a eulogy of Minos, briefly expressed, such as the poet never composed for a single one of the heroes. For that Zeus is a sophist, and that sophistry is a highly honorable art, he makes plain in many other places, and particularly here. For he says that Minos consorted and discoursed with Zeus in the ninth year, and went regularly to be educated by Zeus as though he were a sophist. And the fact that Homer assigned this privilege of having been educated by Zeus to no one among the heroes but Minos makes this a marvellous piece of praise.

greek_head: τοῦτ' ἔστιν ἔπαινος θαυμαστός καὶ Ὀδυσσείας ἐν Νεκυίᾳ δικάζοντα χρυσοῦν σκῆπτρον ἔχοντα πεποίηκε τὸν Μίνων
greek_tokens: outos eimi epainos qaumastos kai odusseia en nekuia dikazw xruseos skhptron exw poiew o minws ou o radamanqun radamanqun de oute entauqa dikazw poiew oute suggignomai o zeus oudamos dia outos fhmi egw minws apas malista upo omhros egkwmiazw o gar zeus eimi pais monos upo zeus paideuw ou exis uperbolh epainos outos gar shmainw o epos o ennewros basileuw zeus megas oarisths
english_head: And in the Ghost-raising in the Odyssey he has described Minos as judging with a golden scepter in his hand, but not Rhadamanthus: Rhadamanthus he has neither described here as judging nor anywhere as consorting with Zeus; wherefore I say that Minos above all persons has been eulogized by Homer. For to have been the son of Zeus, and to have been the only one who was educated by Zeus, is praise unsurpassable. For the meaning of the verse— he was king having colloquy with mighty Zeus in the ninth year” — Hom. Od. 19.179

greek_head: συνουσιαστὴν τοῦ Διὸς εἶναι τὸν Μίνων οἱ γὰρ ὄαροι λόγοι εἰσίν καὶ ὀαριστὴς συνουσιαστής ἐστιν ἐν λόγοις ἐφοίτα
greek_tokens: sunousiasths o zeus eimi o minws e gar oaros logos eimi kai oarisths sunousiasths eimi en logos foitaw oun dia enatos etos eis o o zeus antron o minws o men manqanw o de apodeiknumi o o proteros enneethris manqanw para o zeus eimi de e upolambanw o oarisths sumpoths kai sumpaisths eimi o zeus alla ode an tis tekmhrion xraomai ostis oudeis legw e outws
english_head: is that Minos was a disciple of Zeus. For colloquies are discourses, and he who has colloquy is a disciple by means of discourse. So every ninth year Minos repaired to the cave of Zeus, to learn some things, and to show his knowledge of others that he had learnt from Zeus in the preceding nine years. Some there are who suppose that he who has colloquy is a cup-companion and fellow-jester of Zeus: but one may take the following as a proof that

greek_head: ὑπολαμβάνοντες πολλῶν γὰρ ὄντων ἀνθρώπων καὶ Ἑλλήνων καὶ βαρβάρων οὐκ ἔστιν οἵτινες ἀπέχονται συμποσίων
greek_tokens: upolambanw polus gar eimi anqrwpos kai ellhn kai barbaros ou eimi ostis apexw sumposion kai outos o paidih e eimi oinos allos h krhs kai lakedaimonios deuteros manqanw para krhth en krhth de eis outos eimi o allos nomos os minws tiqhmi mh sumpinw allhlwn eis meqh kaitoi dhlos ostis o nomizw kalos eimi outos nomimos tiqhmi kai o eautou poliths
english_head: they who suppose so are babblers. For of all the many nations of men, both Greek and foreign, the only people who refrain from drinking-bouts and the jesting that occurs where there is wine, are the Cretans, and after them the Spartans, who learnt it from the Cretans. In Crete it is one of their laws which Minos ordained that they are not to drink with each other to intoxication. And yet it is evident that the things he thought honorable were what he ordained as lawful for his people as well. For surely Minos did not, like an inferior person,

greek_head: οὐ γάρ που ὥσπερ γε φαῦλος ἄνθρωπος ὁ Μίνως ἐνόμιζεν μὲν ἕτερα ἐποίει δὲ ἄλλα παρ' ἃ ἐνόμιζεν ἀλλὰ ἦν αὕτη
greek_tokens: ou gar pou wsper ge faulos anqrwpos o minws nomizw men eteros poiew de allos para o nomizw alla eimi outos o sunousia wsper egw legos dia logos epi paideia eis areth oqen dh kai o nomos outos tiqhmi o eautou poliths dia os o te krhth o pas xronos eudaimonew kai lakedaimwn apo e arxw outos xraomai ate qew eimi radamanqus de agaqos men eimi anhr paideuw gar
english_head: think one thing and do another, different from what he thought: no, this intercourse, as I say, was held by means of discussion for education in virtue. Wherefore he ordained for his people these very laws, which have made Crete happy through the length of time, and Sparta happy also, since she began to use them; for they are divine. Rhadamanthus was a good man indeed, for he had been educated by Minos; he had, however, been educated,

greek_head: ὑπὸ τοῦ Μίνω ἐπεπαίδευτο μέντοι οὐχ ὅλην τὴν βασιλικὴν τέχνην ἀλλ' ὑπηρεσίαν τῇ βασιλικῇ ὅσον ἐπιστατεῖν ἐν
greek_tokens: upo o minws paideuw mentoi ou oloc o basilikos texnh alla uphresia o basilikos osos epistatew en o dikasthrion oqen kai dikasths agaqos legw eimi nomofulac gar autos xraomai o minws kata o astu o de kata o allos krhth o talos o gar talos tris perieimi o eniautos kata o kwmh fulassw o nomos en autos en xalkeos grammateion exw grafw o nomos oqen xalkeos kalew erw de kai hsiodos adelfh
english_head: not in the whole of the kingly art, but in one subsidiary to the kingly, enough for presiding in law courts; so that he was spoken of as a good judge. For Minos used him as guardian of the law in the city, and Talos as the same for the rest of Crete. For Talos thrice a year made a round of the villages, guarding the laws in them, by holding their laws inscribed on brazen tablets, which gave him his name of brazen. And what Hesiod also has said

greek_head: τούτων εἰς τὸν Μίνων μνησθεὶς γὰρ αὐτοῦ τοῦ ὀνόματος φησίν
greek_tokens: outos eis o minws mimnhskw gar autos o onoma fhmi os basileios gignomai qnhtos basileus kai pleistos anassw periktiones anqrwpos zeus exw skhptron o kai polew basileuw kai outos legw o o zeus skhptron oudeis allos h o paideia o o zeus h euqunos o krhth dia tis oun pote eimi swkraths outos o fhmh kataskedannumi
english_head: of Minos is akin to this. For after mentioning him by name he remarks— Who was most kingly of mortal kings, and lorded it over more neighboring folk than any, holding the scepter of Zeus: therewith it was that he ruled the cities as king. Hes. Fr. 144And by the scepter of Zeus he means nothing else than the education that he had of Zeus, whereby he directed Crete. Then how has it ever come about, Socrates, that this report is spread abroad of Minos, as an uneducated

greek_head: τοῦ Μίνω ὡς ἀπαιδεύτου τινὸς καὶ χαλεποῦ ὄντος Δι' ὃ καὶ σύ ὦ βέλτιστε ἐὰν σωφρονῇς εὐλαβήσῃ
greek_tokens: o minws ws apaideutos tis kai xalepos eimi dia o kai su eimi beltistos ean swfronew eulabeomai kai allos pas anhr ostis melos o eudokimos eimi mhdepote apexqanomai andris poihtikos mhdeis e gar poihths megas dunamai eis doca epi opoteros an poiew eis o anqrwpos h eulogew h kakhgorew o dh kai ecamartanw o minws polemew ode o polis en h allos te polus sofia eimi kai poihths pantodapos o te allos poihsis
english_head: and harsh-tempered person? Because of something that will make both you, if you are wise, my excellent friend, and everybody else who cares to have a good reputation, beware of ever quarreling with any man of a poetic turn. For poets have great influence over opinion, according as they create it in the minds of men by either commending or vilifying. And this was the mistake that Minos made, in waging war on this city of ours, which besides all its various culture has poets of every kind, and especially those who write tragedy.

greek_head: καὶ τραγῳδίας ἡ δὲ τραγῳδία ἐστὶν παλαιὸν ἐνθάδε οὐχ ὡς οἴονται ἀπὸ Θέσπιδος ἀρξαμένη οὐδ' ἀπὸ Φρυνίχου ἀλλ'
greek_tokens: kai tragwdia o de tragwdia eimi palaios enqade ou ws oiomai apo qespis arxw oude apo frunixos alla ei eqelw ennoew panu palaios autos eurhsis eimi ode o polis eurhma eimi de o poihsis dhmoterphs te kai yuxagwgikos o tragwdia en h dh kai enteinw egw o minws timwrew anti os egw anagkazw o dasmos tellw ekeinos outos oun ecamartanw o minws apexqomai egw oqen dh o su erwtaw kakodocos
english_head: Now tragedy is a thing of ancient standing here; it did not begin, as people suppose, from Thespis or from Phrynicus, but if you will reflect, you will find it is a very ancient invention of our city. Tragedy is the most popularly delightful and soul-enthralling branch of poetry: in it, accordingly, we get Minos on the rack of verse,, and thus avenge ourselves for that tribute which he compelled us to pay This, then, was the mistake that Minos made—his quarrel with us—and hence it is that, as you said in your question, he has fallen more and more into evil repute. For that he was a good

greek_head: γέγονεν ἐπεὶ ὅτι γε ἀγαθὸς ἦν καὶ νόμιμος ὅπερ καὶ ἐν τοῖς πρόσθεν ἐλέγομεν νομεὺς ἀγαθός τοῦτο μέγιστον σημεῖον
greek_tokens: gignomai epei ostis ge agaqos eimi kai nomimos osper kai en o prosqen legw nomeus agaqos outos megas shmeion ostis akinhtos autos e nomos eimi ate o eimi peri polis oikhsis eceuriskw eu o alhqeia dokew egw eimi swkraths eoika o logos erw oukoun ei egw alhqhs legos dokew su palaios krhs e minws kai radamanquos poliths nomos xraomai fainw outos ara o palaios aristos nomoqeths gignomai
english_head: and law-abiding person, as we stated in what went before—a good apportioner—is most convincingly shown by the fact the his laws are unshaken, since they were made by one who discovered aright the truth of reality in regard to the management of a state. In my opinion, Socrates, your statement is a probable one. Then if what I say is true, do you consider that the Cretan people of Minos and Rhadamanthus use the most ancient laws? I do. So these have shown themselves the best lawgivers among men of ancient times—

greek_head: νομῆς τε καὶ ποιμένες ἀνδρῶν ὥσπερ καὶ Ὅμηρος ἔφη ποιμένα λαῶν εἶναι τὸν ἀγαθὸν στρατηγόν
greek_tokens: nomazw te kai poimhn androw wsper kai omhros fhmi poimhn laos eimi o agaqos strathgos panu men oun ferw dh pros zeus filios ei tis egw eromai o o swma agaqos nomoqeths te kai nomeus tis eimi outos o dianemw epi o swma beltiwn autos poiew eipon an kalos te kai dia braxea apokrinw ostis trofh te kai ponos o men aucanw o de gumnazw kai sunisthmi o swma autos orqos ge
english_head: apportioners and shepherds of men; just as Homer called the good general a shepherd of the folk. Quite so, indeed. Come then, in good friendship’s name: if someone should ask us what it is that the good lawgiver and apportioner for the body distributes to it when he makes it better, we should say, if we were to make a correct and brief answer, that it was food and labor; the former to strengthen, and the latter to exercise and brace it. And we should be right.

greek_head: Εἰ οὖν δὴ μετὰ τοῦτο ἔροιτο ἡμᾶς Τί δὲ δή ποτε ἐκεῖνά ἐστιν ἃ ὁ ἀγαθὸς νομοθέτης τε καὶ νομεὺς διανέμων
greek_tokens: ei oun dh meta outos eromai egw tis de dh pote ekeinos eimi o o agaqos nomoqeths te kai nomeus dianemw epi o yuxh beltiwn autos poiew tis an apokrinw ou an aisxunw kai uper egw autos kai o hlikia eautou ouketi outos exw eipon alla mentoi aisxros ge o yuxw egw eimi ekateros o men en autos fainw mh oida en os autos eneimi kai o agaqos kai o flauros o de o swma kai o o allos skeptomai
english_head: And if he then proceeded to ask us—And what might that be which the good lawgiver and apportioner distributes to the soul to make it better?—what would be our answer if we would avoid being ashamed of ourselves and our years? This time I am unable to say. But indeed it is shameful for the soul of either of us to be found ignorant of those things within it on which its good and abject states depend, while it has studied those that pertain to the body and rest.
